title: Generative AI at work: Survey evidence from three Central Banks
study_design_description: In spring 2024, surveys on generative AI in the workplace were conducted in partnership with three central banks: the National Bank of Slovakia, the Czech National Bank, and the European Central Bank. The survey was designed to understand key questions regarding generative AI use within these samples, focusing on large language models as the most applicable generative AI tool for central banking work. The sample from the National Bank of Slovakia covers employees throughout the entire organizational hierarchy, while the samples from the Czech National Bank and the European Central Bank focus on a specific subgroup: PhD economists in central banks, surveyed as part of a seminar on how economists can use generative AI.
primary_task_description: Participants were asked about their use of generative AI across various workplace tasks. Both samples were asked about 10 general tasks where generative AI can be helpful: (i) writing emails or reports, (ii) generating code or debugging, (iii) creating content (articles, blogs, etc), (iv) data analysis and visualization, (v) educational purposes or learning new skills, (vi) customer service, (vii) research and information gathering, (viii) design and creative work, and (x) other. The ECB sample additionally included workplace tasks specifically related to the work done by PhD economists, including conducting literature reviews, cleaning data and preprocessing, exploratory data analysis, statistical modeling and econometric analysis, generating economic models/simulations/forecasts, writing research papers or reports, drafting policy recommendations, explaining economic concepts or theories, answering research questions, peer review or providing feedback, research ideation and brainstorming, coding or programming tasks, creating presentations or teaching materials, and project or task management. For each task, participants reported whether they have used generative AI for the task, which generative AI tool(s) they have used, and how many hours in an average week they used generative AI tools for the task.
population_description: The sample was drawn from three central banks: the European Central Bank (ECB), the National Bank of Slovakia (NBS), and the Czech National Bank (CNB). The sample is highly well-educated, with over 90 percent of participants having a master's degree or higher. 37 percent of the sample is female, and 15 percent are managers. The average respondent is 41 years old and has spent 9 years at their organization. On average participants work for 45 hours a week. Almost a third of their work time is spent on analytical tasks, a quarter on non-routine cognitive tasks, and just under 20 percent on routine cognitive tasks. The remaining quarter of their time is spent primarily on leadership and management tasks, and interpersonal and communication tasks.

notes: This is a survey-based observational study with no experimental manipulation or control group. The paper examines generative AI adoption and usage patterns across three central banks (National Bank of Slovakia, Czech National Bank, and European Central Bank) through employee surveys conducted in spring 2024. The study includes both a broad organizational sample (NBS, N=162) and a specialized sample of PhD economists (ECB and CNB combined, N=183), with a total sample of N=345. The paper does not compare AI vs. no-AI conditions but rather examines correlates and predictors of voluntary adoption among workers. For the ECB sample, some survey questions were dropped to prevent participant fatigue, so data on which tools were used for economics tasks is not available for that subsample. The paper notes that privacy concerns are not the primary obstacles preventing widespread adoption; instead, almost 95% of central bankers identify the lack of clear use cases as the main cause. Productivity impact figures are self-reported beliefs rather than measured outcomes.
